own_review.point: Stale KDoc: commonMain Builder docs for maxAge/maxStale/minFresh still reference [TimeUnit.SECONDS], which does not exist/resolve in common code (broken Dokka links on non-JVM); should be DurationUnit.SECONDS

API duplication / design: JVM now exposes both maxAge(Int, TimeUnit) and actual maxAge(Int, DurationUnit) with no deprecation or migration guidance (the human reviewer's 'stuck doubling our methods' concern); idiomatic multiplatform API would be maxAge(kotlin.time.Duration) instead of Int+DurationUnit
commonClampToInt is a generic Long->Int clamp placed in -CacheControlCommon.kt rather than -UtilCommon.kt, where the analogous toNonNegativeInt clamping helper already lives

Test gap: commonTest only exercises DurationUnit.SECONDS (identity case); no common-path test for unit conversion (e.g. MINUTES->60s), saturation/clamp to Int.MAX_VALUE, or negative rejection through the new toDuration().inWholeSeconds path (JVM tests only cover the TimeUnit overload)
Missed follow-up: FORCE_CACHE remains commented out in commonMain even though its only dependency (maxStale) is now available in common; it could be uncommented/commonized

Verified correct: behavior parity with old JVM impl (truncation, Duration saturation handles overflow, require>=0 preserved); DurationUnit is a distinct enum in Kotlin 1.6.10 so no JVM overload signature clash; TimeUnit overloads retained so no source/binary break

points.point: Dual TimeUnit/DurationUnit overloads on JVM with no deprecation, split API surface
points.why: Verified: both overloads coexist undeprecated; matches the human reviewer's central 'doubling our methods' concern

points.point: FORCE_CACHE constant still built with legacy TimeUnit overload, update for consistency
points.why: Verified at jvmMain CacheControl.kt:183 (.maxStale(Integer.MAX_VALUE, TimeUnit.SECONDS)); concrete actionable consistency fix

points.point: KDoc on expect declaration still references TimeUnit.SECONDS instead of DurationUnit
points.why: Verified: commonMain KDoc keeps [TimeUnit.SECONDS], a broken reference in common code

points.point: commonClampToInt lacks lower-bound clamp, suggest defense-in-depth
points.why: Self-acknowledged as acceptable; require(>=0) guards all callers, no real issue

points.point: JVM tests only exercise TimeUnit overloads; add DurationUnit variant tests
points.why: Verified: CacheControlJvmTest.kt uses only TimeUnit; new common path under-tested

points.point: commonTest only tests DurationUnit.SECONDS identity; add a conversion-unit test
points.why: Verified real coverage gap in the new cross-platform conversion logic

points.point: Cache.kt KDoc examples reference TimeUnit overloads, update if deprecating
points.why: Verified: Cache.kt lines 98 and 133 use .maxAge(0, TimeUnit.SECONDS) / .maxStale(365, TimeUnit.DAYS)

missed: Idiomatic multiplatform design alternative: maxAge(kotlin.time.Duration) instead of Int+DurationUnit
FORCE_CACHE could now be uncommented/commonized in commonMain since maxStale is available in common
exceeds: Cache.kt KDoc examples still showing TimeUnit usage (verified, neither human nor judge found)
FORCE_CACHE companion constant built via legacy TimeUnit overload (verified, neither human nor judge found)

points.point: KDoc still references TimeUnit.SECONDS, should be DurationUnit.SECONDS
points.why: Verified stale/broken doc reference in commonMain

points.point: commonClampToInt doesn't guard negatives, 'would silently wrap to positive Int'
points.why: Callers guard with require(>=0); the wrap-to-positive claim is imprecise (small negatives stay negative), purely defensive musing

points.point: JVM retains both overloads correctly - good
Verify non-JVM actual implementations are included (diff truncated)
points.why: Hedge, not a finding; the nonJvm actuals ARE in the commit (verified via git), so nothing actionable

points.point: Possible overflow with NANOSECONDS in toDuration().inWholeSeconds, safe due to clamp, add comment
points.why: Duration saturates rather than overflows; concedes result is safe, suggestion is only a comment

missed: Dual TimeUnit/DurationUnit API surface on JVM with no deprecation (the human's main point)
Test coverage gap: only DurationUnit.SECONDS exercised, no conversion/clamp/negative tests for the new common path
FORCE_CACHE could now be commonized since maxStale is common

points.point: KDoc for all three methods still references [TimeUnit.SECONDS], update to DurationUnit
points.why: Verified stale/broken doc reference in commonMain

points.point: commonClampToInt clamps only upper bound, add explanatory comment
points.why: Correctly notes it is safe; only suggests a comment, no real issue

points.point: Tests use only small SECONDS values; add clamping, negative-throw, and large-value edge cases
points.why: Verified real coverage gap; sub-claim (c) that Int.MAX_VALUE seconds 'overflows Long in toDuration()' is wrong (fits comfortably, Duration saturates anyway), but the core test-gap point stands

points.point: FORCE_CACHE still commented out in expect declaration; now uncommentable since maxStale is multiplatform
points.why: Verified: commonMain has FORCE_CACHE commented out, only JVM defines it (jvmMain:181); maxStale dependency now satisfied in common

points.point: TimeUnit and DurationUnit overloads coexist; consider @Deprecated on TimeUnit variants per file convention
points.why: Verified dual API surface; matches human's 'doubling our methods' concern and cites the file's actual deprecation convention

missed: Idiomatic multiplatform design alternative: maxAge(kotlin.time.Duration) instead of Int+DurationUnit

qualitative: v2 is the best review: it makes the human's central point (dual TimeUnit/DurationUnit API surface with no deprecation), catches the stale KDoc and both test-coverage gaps, and adds two verified findings nobody else had (Cache.kt KDoc examples and the FORCE_CACHE constant still using the legacy overload). v4 is a close second with a tighter, fully accurate set: it uniquely connects the now-common maxStale to the still-commented FORCE_CACHE in commonMain and grounds its deprecation suggestion in the file's existing convention, though one test sub-claim about Long overflow in toDuration() is wrong. v3 is clearly the weakest: it finds only the KDoc issue, pads with praise and an unactionable 'verify the truncated diff' hedge, mischaracterizes both clamping (wrap-to-positive) and Duration overflow (it saturates), and completely misses the human's dual-API concern. Characteristic across all three: none questioned the Int+DurationUnit shape itself versus an idiomatic kotlin.time.Duration parameter, and none had false positives serious enough to score wrong.
